title: St. George (Monthly) — St. Leontius of Tripoli & St. Marina of Antioch
narrative:
The Monthly Commemoration of the Holy Great Martyr George the Trophy-Bearer, St. Leontius the Soldier-Martyr of Tripoli, and St. Marina the Great Martyr of Antioch

On the eighteenth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the holy, glorious Great Martyr George the Trophy-Bearer, the Cappadocian soldier-saint who is among the most universally venerated of all the martyrs in the Eastern and Ethiopian traditions and whose feast returns monthly in the Tewahedo calendar as the perpetual celebration of the military virtue of the faith — the courage that does not flinch before power, the refusal of the soul to bow to what the body is threatened into bowing to, the declaration that there are things worth dying for and that the soldier who has once fought for an emperor can discover that the only war worth fighting is the one that has already been won at the Cross. George is celebrated in the Ethiopian tradition with particular devotion, his icon prominent in every church, his intercession sought in every danger, his feast marked with a joy that goes beyond ordinary liturgical celebration into something more like the welcome given to a beloved member of the household who has returned after a long absence.

The theology of George in the Tewahedo tradition is a theology of the transformed warrior: George does not lay down his martial gifts when he becomes a martyr but redirects them, and the tradition's image of him as the dragon-slayer is the iconographic expression of this redirection. The dragon that George slays is not merely a fairy-tale monster but the great serpent of the Book of Revelation, the ancient enemy of the human race, and George's victory over it is the martyr's participation in Christ's victory over death: the soldier who would not sacrifice to the gods participates in the sacrifice that has already abolished all sacrifices, and the lance that kills the dragon is the same lance that pierced the side of Christ on the Cross, from which flowed the blood and water of sacramental life. The monthly return of George's feast is the community's monthly renewal of its participation in this cosmic victory, its monthly declaration that it stands with the Trophy-Bearer against every power that demands of it what only God deserves.

Saint Leontius of Tripoli is a soldier-martyr of the third century whose account in the tradition emphasises the particular dynamic of the military martyr: a man whose entire professional formation was oriented toward obedience, toward the execution of commands without question, who then discovered that there was one command he could not execute — the command to deny Christ — and who died for that discovery. Leontius was tortured with a refinement designed specifically to break the will of a soldier: he was made to march barefoot across burning ground, to endure what the tradition describes as multiple sessions of systematic torment, and yet his refusal was absolute and his composure throughout was such that he converted Hypatius and Theodulus, two of the soldiers assigned to guard him, who were themselves then martyred alongside him. Leontius is the tradition's emblem of the contagion of sanctity: the martyr whose bearing in suffering is so evidently the bearing of a man indwelt by a power greater than pain that those closest to him are drawn into the same witness.

Saint Marina of Antioch — known in the Western tradition as Saint Margaret — is one of the most beloved of the virgin-martyrs, a young woman whose story includes one of the tradition's most vivid supernatural episodes: imprisoned for her refusal to marry the prefect Olybrius, Marina was swallowed by Satan in the form of a dragon and burst forth from him unharmed, a narrative the tradition reads as the icon of the martyr's passage through death into resurrection — the dragon that swallows cannot digest the one who belongs to Christ, and the woman who emerges from the belly of the beast emerges unchanged and victorious. Marina's death by beheading followed these supernatural encounters, and her story spread rapidly across the Christian world to become one of the most widely celebrated of all the martyrdom narratives. The eighteenth day of Sene thus assembles three military and martial witnesses around the monthly feast of the great Trophy-Bearer: the soldier who converted his guards, and the young woman who burst unharmed from the dragon's belly, all three witnesses to the single truth that George embodies in his iconic lance-thrust — that the power of Christ is greater than every power that opposes it. Amen.
